mission.title: Install and Configure Google Kubernetes Engine (GKE)
mission.description: Google Kubernetes Engine (GKE) is a managed Kubernetes service that simplifies the deployment, management, and scaling of containerized applications using Kubernetes. It is ideal for users who want to leverage Kubernetes without the overhead of managing the underlying infrastructure. Key trade-offs include reliance on Google Cloud services and potential costs associated with resource usage.

steps.title: Install the Google Cloud SDK
steps.description:
The Google Cloud SDK provides the gcloud command-line tool to interact with GKE.

```bash

curl https://sdk.cloud.google.com | bash

exec -l $SHELL

gcloud init

```

> This command installs the Google Cloud SDK.
> Reload the shell to use the gcloud command.
> Initialize the gcloud environment and authenticate.

steps.title: Create a GKE Cluster
steps.description:
This step creates a GKE cluster with the specified Kubernetes version, node count, and region.

```bash

gcloud container clusters create my-gke-cluster --num-nodes=3 --zone=us-central1-a --cluster-version=1.31

```

> Replace 'my-gke-cluster' with your desired cluster name.

steps.title: Set Up kubeconfig
steps.description:
Configure kubectl to use the new GKE cluster by updating the kubeconfig file.

```bash

gcloud container clusters get-credentials my-gke-cluster --zone=us-central1-a

```

> This command fetches cluster credentials.

steps.title: Verify Cluster Nodes
steps.description:
Check the status of the nodes in the GKE cluster to ensure they are running.

```bash

kubectl get nodes

```

> You should see the nodes listed with a 'Ready' status.

steps.title: Post-Install Configuration
steps.description:
Set up monitoring and autoscaling for your GKE cluster.

```bash

gcloud container clusters update my-gke-cluster --enable-autoscaling --min-nodes=1 --max-nodes=5 --zone=us-central1-a

gcloud services enable monitoring.googleapis.com

```

> Enables autoscaling for the cluster.
> Enables Google Cloud Monitoring for the cluster.

uninstall.title: Delete the GKE Cluster
uninstall.description:
This command will delete the GKE cluster and all associated resources, which may result in data loss.

```bash

gcloud container clusters delete my-gke-cluster --zone=us-central1-a --quiet

```

> Use --quiet to skip confirmation prompts.

uninstall.title: Clean Up Orphaned Resources
uninstall.description:
Check for and manually delete any orphaned resources such as load balancers or persistent disks.

```bash

gcloud compute addresses list

gcloud compute disks list

```

> List any static IPs that may need to be deleted.
> List any persistent disks that may need to be deleted.

upgrade.title: Upgrade the GKE Cluster
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, ensure you back up any important data and verify the current node status.

```bash

kubectl get nodes

gcloud container clusters upgrade my-gke-cluster --cluster-version=1.31 --zone=us-central1-a

kubectl get nodes

```

> Check the status of all nodes before proceeding.
> This command upgrades the cluster to the specified version.
> Verify that all nodes are back to 'Ready' status after the upgrade.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Unable to connect to the server: x.x.x.x'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This usually indicates that the kubeconfig is not set up correctly or the cluster is not reachable.

**Fix:**
Run 'gcloud container clusters get-credentials my-gke-cluster --zone=us-central1-a' to update kubeconfig.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Insufficient permissions'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** The user account does not have the necessary IAM permissions to perform the action.

**Fix:**
Ensure your user account has the 'Kubernetes Engine Admin' role assigned.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'No resources found'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This can occur if the namespace is incorrect or if resources have not been created.

**Fix:**
Check the namespace with 'kubectl get pods --all-namespaces' to verify resource existence.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Cluster is not running'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** The GKE cluster may be in a state of maintenance or has been deleted.

**Fix:**
Check the cluster status with 'gcloud container clusters list' and ensure it is running.

versionNotes.changes: Added support for PodSecurity admission, improved integration with Anthos.
versionNotes.deprecations: Deprecated legacy network policies, removed support for Kubernetes 1.24.
versionNotes.migrationSteps: If upgrading from 1.30, review the PodSecurity admission documentation for configuration changes.

resolution.summary: You should see a list of nodes with 'Ready' status when running 'kubectl get nodes'.

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have the necessary tools and resources before starting the installation.
